item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is simple - burning snakes. By the way you tried to present it, I think you intended to give off a poetic wrath-of-the-nature vibe, climaxing at the beautiful interview log in the end of the article.

Sadly, I don't think it worked. You failed to get the clinical tone necessary for the containment procedure and description by putting in too many strange words ("quarry", "outed") in place of what could have been more easily understood technical terms. It also gets too wordy and a bit exhausting to read.

Neutral vote, because of the great interview log with Tyson H████. You should write a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed the concept.
Initially from the first interview I suspected that the man was actually an amalgamation of the snakes and this was an as yet, undiscovered anomaly.
I can't say I was disappointed at the end but it could have maybe been a neater end

I do think containment needs to be updated
If you are saying that one snake can ignite and "burn" at temps above 300°C and when they join together this increases, there isn't really such thing as fire proof mesh as mesh is welded together and the welds would melt. Perforated steel sheet might be a better shout.

Upvoted as I liked the idea

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is too wordy for me.

For instance, where you say:

Personnel found infringing this directive are to be severely reprimanded.

You might instead put:

Violations will be severely reprimanded

Or something else which fits your ideas better and rolls off the tongue a little easier.

Get the word count down. I found myself getting bored until I hit the last interview (which I think is very good at the beginning in an "omg, I can feel a twist coming but have no idea what it is going to be" sort of way).

Good luck!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, I am quite conflicted here. I actually don't mind the ending. However, the interviews are problematic. The first one feels irrelevant. The second feels off. Did the person come to his conclusion independent of the SCP? But I don't see why a pair of fire snakes would shift his worldview anymore than an actual fire?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Next time try something like. SCP-3102 memetic abilities or anomaly comes to show when treatened.
It would add some level of profesionalism to it but it was a good scp non the less

I will have to disagree with your statement. Simply slotting in the words 'memetic' or 'anomalous' does not convey professionalism automatically. The current wording is fine, as it is. It simply describes a standard observation.

Furthermore, why do you suggest 'memetic'? I don't see any rationale that this SCP can be memetic in any way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The one thing i'd change is the way you introduced this scp's anomaly. Next time try something like. SCP-3102 memetic abilities or anomaly comes to show when treatened.
It would add some level of profesionalism to it but it was a good scp non the less
Cheers

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't taken at all with the anomaly. The behaviour of the snakes doesn't make zoological sense, and is obviously only to make them more dangerous. The first interview is redundant, as noted above.

You almost won me over with the second interview - Tyrone's dialogue is purple but it's a pretty well written example of the style. But the "snakes burst out, people die" ending is such a cliche, and makes even less sense than before, sealing my downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most of the article was kinda 'ehh', with me leaning towards a downvote, but the last interview log was what killed it for me. This man's dialogue is to purply for me too take him seriously. The motivation for why he's doing what he's doing is fine, if a little generic, but that tone is really killing it for me.

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a few things which I like and a few things which I don't about this, which balances out to a neutral vote.

I think you pull off the image of fire snakes about as well as can be expected; the details of its behavior helped in that regard. I also liked the voice of Tyson a lot—the interview gave me a kind of True Detective vibe, in a good way. Tyson is the subtle sort of crazy that's harder to pull off. I also felt like the ending was pretty strong; there's enough hinting at something bigger to be effective.

On the downside, I think this suffers from a lot of pacing issues. The first interview feels largely redundant because we already know that the snakes do these things. The Tyson interview log is similarly long; I'm not sure you need to have him go on at such length.

item.title: (no title)
